meta.description: Coaching tips for Level 6: Multi-Agent Parallelism

tips.tip: Look at your current task: which parts have NO data dependency on each other? Those can run in parallel AI sessions.
tips.example_before: Build API → then frontend → then tests [serial, one at a time]
tips.example_after: API + Frontend can start in parallel (shared interface contract). Tests for each layer run in a third parallel session.

tips.tip: A good parallelism test: if Task A's output doesn't change Task B's implementation, they can run in parallel.
tips.example_before: I'll do these one at a time to be safe
tips.example_after: User model + Auth middleware + Email templates — these three have no shared state, they can all start now.

tips.tip: Start with 2-3 parallel agents, not 5+. You need to be able to review all outputs. More agents = more merge conflicts.
tips.example_before: Let me spin up 6 agents for maximum speed
tips.example_after: 3 agents: one for API, one for frontend components, one for test infrastructure. I can review all three without context-switching overhead.

tips.tip: Use `git worktree` to run multiple Claude Code instances on separate branches — no conflicts, easy merging.
tips.example_before: Switching branches back and forth, stashing changes, losing context
tips.example_after:
git worktree add ../project-api feature/api
git worktree add ../project-ui feature/ui
# Two separate directories, two Claude instances, zero conflicts

tips.tip: Worktree workflow: create worktree → open Claude → complete task → create PR → remove worktree. Clean and isolated.
tips.example_before: All work on one branch, manual conflict resolution
tips.example_after:
git worktree add ../feat-search feature/search
cd ../feat-search && claude  # work in isolation
git push && gh pr create  # PR when done
git worktree remove ../feat-search

tips.tip: Before starting parallel work, define the interface contracts: API shapes, function signatures, data types that cross boundaries.
tips.example_before: Start frontend and API in parallel, realize they disagree on the response format
tips.example_after: First: define the API response type. Share it between frontend and backend. Now both can build against the same contract.

tech_variants.react: Define TypeScript interfaces in a shared types/ directory. Frontend and API both import from there.
tech_variants.python: Define Pydantic models in a shared schemas/ module. API and client both validate against the same schema.
tech_variants.go: Define Protocol Buffer or OpenAPI spec first. Generate client and server code from the same source.

tips.tip: Write contract tests for your shared interfaces. When parallel branches merge, contract tests catch integration mismatches immediately.
tips.example_before: Merge two branches, discover API returns {items: [...]} but frontend expects {data: [...]}
tips.example_after: Contract test: API response must match ProductListResponse type. Fails immediately if either side changes the shape.

tips.tip: When managing multiple agents, keep a simple tracking note: which agent is doing what, what's the status, what needs review.
tips.example_before: Three Claude instances open, lost track of which one is doing what
tips.example_after:
Agent 1: API endpoints [done, needs review]
Agent 2: React components [in progress]
Agent 3: Test suite [blocked on Agent 1 API types]
